content.text: What Are Data Contracts for AI Pipelines?

A data contract is a formal, machine-readable agreement between a data producer and a data consumer that specifies the schema, semantics, quality expectations, and operational terms of a dataset. In enterprise AI pipelines, data contracts govern the interface between the systems that produce training data, evaluation datasets, and grounding sources, and the AI pipelines that consume them.

Without data contracts, AI pipelines are tightly coupled to implicit assumptions about their input data: field names, data types, null rates, and value distributions. When producers change their data without notice, AI pipelines silently degrade or fail. Data contracts make these assumptions explicit, version-controlled, and testable. They shift data interface changes from unexpected incidents to planned, negotiated operations.
Data Contract Schema Standards
The diagram below shows the structure of a data contract for an AI pipeline grounding source, with the producer, contract registry, and two consumer pipelines.

attrs.alt: Data contract registry architecture showing producer publishing schema to registry, contract consumed by RAG grounding and fine-tuning pipelines, contract monitoring layer blocking ingestion on violation

content.text: The contract registry is the authoritative source for all contract versions. Each consumer pipeline subscribes to a specific contract version. When the producer updates the schema, it publishes a new contract version; consumers opt in to the new version on their own schedule, enabling independent deployment of producers and consumers.
Anatomy of a Data Contract
A data contract for an AI pipeline grounding source has five sections: schema, quality expectations, SLA terms, ownership, and versioning metadata.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Literal

@dataclass
class FieldSpec:
    name: str
    dtype: str                    # 'str', 'int', 'float', 'datetime', 'bool'
    nullable: bool
    description: str
    pii_category: str | None      # None if not PII; 'name', 'email', etc. if PII
    example: str | None           # Representative example value

@dataclass
class QualityExpectations:
    completeness_threshold: float  # Min fraction of non-null values per delivery (0–1)
    freshness_max_age_hours: float  # Max age of most-recent record at delivery time
    validity_rules: list[str]      # Plain-English descriptions of validation rules
    row_count_min: int             # Minimum expected rows per delivery

@dataclass
class SLATerms:
    delivery_frequency: str        # 'hourly', 'daily', 'on-change', etc.
    max_delivery_latency_minutes: int  # Time from source update to delivery
    incident_notification_sla_minutes: int  # Time to notify consumers on breach

@dataclass
class DataContract:
    id: str
    version: str                   # Semantic versioning: 'v1.2.0'
    name: str
    description: str
    producer: str                  # Team or system name
    consumers: list[str]           # Pipeline names that depend on this contract
    steward: str                   # Named accountable owner
    schema: list[FieldSpec]
    quality: QualityExpectations
    sla: SLATerms
    effective_date: datetime
    expiry_date: datetime | None
    changelog: list[str]           # Human-readable summary of changes per version

The pii_category field in FieldSpec is essential for AI pipelines: a field that enters the RAG index must have its PII category declared so that the ingestion pipeline can apply appropriate masking or exclusion before embedding. Undeclared PII that flows into a vector store is a compliance failure.
Schema Validation at Ingestion Time

Every delivery from a producer must be validated against the contract's schema before entering the AI pipeline. Schema validation at ingestion time prevents malformed or type-mismatched data from corrupting embeddings or producing incorrect agent responses downstream.

When validation fails, the ingestion pipeline must block the batch and trigger a breach notification to the producer. It must not ingest partial batches—partial ingestion creates a gap in the grounding corpus that is invisible to monitoring until a retrieval miss surfaces it.
Contract Versioning and Backward Compatibility
Data contracts must follow semantic versioning. The versioning rules define what constitutes a breaking vs. non-breaking change, and therefore what kind of producer-consumer coordination is required.
Change Type
Version Bump
Consumer Coordination Required
Add optional (nullable) field
MINOR (v1.1.0)
None — consumers can ignore new field
Add non-nullable required field
MAJOR (v2.0.0)
All consumers must update before producer deploys
Rename field
MAJOR (v2.0.0)
All consumers must update; migration period with old+new both present
Change data type
MAJOR (v2.0.0)
All consumers must update; type coercion may corrupt AI pipeline state
Remove field
MAJOR (v2.0.0)
Deprecate in MINOR first; hard remove only after all consumers confirmed updated
Tighten validity rule
PATCH (v1.0.1)
Notify consumers; tighter rules may cause new validation failures
PII and Sensitive Data Declarations
AI pipeline data contracts must explicitly declare the PII category of every field. This declaration drives downstream treatment in the ingestion pipeline, the vector store, and the retrieval layer.

PII field masking: Fields with pii_category != None must be masked or tokenized before embedding. The masking function should be defined in the contract itself, not hardcoded in the ingestion pipeline, so that masking strategy is versioned alongside the schema.

Selective field exclusion: Some PII fields should be excluded entirely from the AI pipeline rather than masked. For example, a customer phone number that is used for verification but is not relevant to any AI use case should not enter the embedding pipeline at all. The contract can flag these fields with exclude_from_ai: true.

Right-to-erasure propagation: When a data subject exercises their right to erasure, the contract's pii_category declarations determine which fields must be purged from the vector store. The contract registry provides the source of truth for identifying which chunks in the vector store contain PII for a given subject.

Consent alignment: If a field is governed by a consent record (e.g., a customer's preference data that requires explicit AI processing consent), the contract should reference the consent category. The ingestion pipeline checks the active consent records before including that field in the embedding batch.
Contract Registry Implementation

The contract registry is the operational backbone of the data contract standard. It must support four operations: register a new contract, publish a new version, look up the contract for a given producer-consumer pair, and query all contracts that a consumer pipeline depends on.

The list_consumer_contracts method enables a pipeline to discover all contracts it depends on at startup and register schema validators for each. This is the foundation for automated contract compliance monitoring: the pipeline validates each incoming batch against all of its registered contracts without requiring manual configuration of which contract to apply to which data source.
Quality Expectation Monitoring

Schema validity is a necessary but not sufficient condition for data contract compliance. A delivery can be schema-valid (all fields present, correct types) while still violating quality expectations: too many nulls, values outside defined ranges, records that are older than the freshness SLA. Quality expectation monitoring runs after schema validation on every delivery batch.

from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class QualityCheckResult:
    passed: bool
    metric: str
    actual: float
    threshold: float
    message: str

def check_quality(
    records: list[dict[str, Any]],
    contract: DataContract,
    now: datetime | None = None,
) -> list[QualityCheckResult]:
    """Run quality checks against contract quality expectations."""
    if now is None:
        now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)

    results: list[QualityCheckResult] = []
    q = contract.quality
    n = len(records)
    if n == 0:
        return [QualityCheckResult(
            passed=n >= q.row_count_min, metric="row_count",
            actual=0, threshold=q.row_count_min,
            message=f"Empty delivery; minimum {q.row_count_min} rows required",
        )]

    # Row count check
    results.append(QualityCheckResult(
        passed=n >= q.row_count_min, metric="row_count",
        actual=n, threshold=q.row_count_min,
        message=f"{n} rows delivered; minimum {q.row_count_min}",
    ))

    # Completeness check per non-nullable field
    for spec in contract.schema:
        if spec.nullable:
            continue
        non_null = sum(1 for r in records if r.get(spec.name) is not None)
        completeness = non_null / n
        results.append(QualityCheckResult(
            passed=completeness >= q.completeness_threshold,
            metric=f"completeness:{spec.name}",
            actual=completeness,
            threshold=q.completeness_threshold,
            message=f"Field '{spec.name}': {completeness:.1%} non-null",
        ))

    # Freshness check: look for a 'updated_at' or 'modified_at' timestamp field
    ts_fields = [s.name for s in contract.schema if s.dtype == 'datetime']
    for ts_field in ts_fields:
        timestamps = [
            r[ts_field] for r in records
            if isinstance(r.get(ts_field), datetime)
        ]
        if timestamps:
            max_ts = max(timestamps)
            age_hours = (now - max_ts.replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc)).total_seconds() / 3600
            results.append(QualityCheckResult(
                passed=age_hours <= q.freshness_max_age_hours,
                metric=f"freshness:{ts_field}",
                actual=age_hours,
                threshold=q.freshness_max_age_hours,
                message=f"Most recent '{ts_field}': {age_hours:.1f}h ago",
            ))

    return results

The completeness check per field is more useful than a single aggregate completeness score because different fields have different criticality. A 98% completeness rate for a description field may be acceptable, but 98% completeness for a price field means 2% of products will be grounded with a missing price—which causes the agent to either refuse to answer or fabricate.
Handling Schema Drift at Runtime

Schema drift occurs when the producer's actual output silently diverges from the declared contract schema without a formal version update. This is the most common and most dangerous contract violation in practice: the producer team changes their data model, forgets to update the contract, and the AI pipeline begins receiving data it was not designed to handle. Schema drift manifests as extra fields, missing fields, or type changes that pass JSON parsing but fail downstream logic.

Extra fields detection: The contract validator should flag records that contain fields not declared in the contract schema. Extra fields are not necessarily harmful to the current pipeline but indicate that the producer has added something the contract does not cover. Undeclared fields may be PII that the pipeline would now inadvertently embed. Extra field warnings should be escalated to the data steward for a contract update.

Missing required fields: A previously populated required field that disappears in a new delivery is a hard schema violation. The validator must block the batch. This typically indicates that the producer removed a field from their output schema without publishing a new contract version.

Type coercion risk: When a field's data type changes silently (e.g., a price field changes from float to string because of a currency symbol), schema-only validation may miss it if the validator only checks JSON type rather than domain type. Implement domain-specific validation rules (price must be a positive finite float) as explicit validity rules in the QualityExpectations section.

Drift alerting: Log a schema drift event every time a delivery contains extra fields, type mismatches, or missing fields. Send a breach notification to the producer's on-call contact immediately. Configure the AI pipeline's ingestion job to accept a configurable number of consecutive violations before entering a hard block state, to avoid cascading failures from transient data issues.
Contract Testing and CI Integration

Data contracts should be tested in the producer's CI pipeline, not only at runtime. Contract tests run against a sample of the producer's output data and verify that the schema, quality expectations, and SLA terms would be satisfied by the current implementation. A failing contract test is a build failure.

Schema conformance test: Generate a batch of sample records from the producer system. Run the contract validator against the batch. Assert that ValidationResult.passed is True and that no unexpected fields are present.

Quality threshold test: Compute the completeness and validity metrics for the sample batch. Assert that they exceed the contract's quality thresholds. If the production system has known data quality issues, these tests expose them before they reach the AI pipeline.

Backward compatibility test: When the producer changes its output schema, run the existing contract validator (current version) against the new output. If validation passes without modification, the change is backward compatible. If it fails, the producer must either fix the output or publish a new major contract version and coordinate with consumers before deploying.
Key Takeaways
Data contracts make implicit schema and quality assumptions explicit and versioned; without them, AI pipelines are silently broken by producer changes.
A well-formed contract has five sections: schema (fields, types, nullability), quality expectations (completeness, freshness, validity), SLA terms, ownership, and versioning metadata.
Schema validation must run at ingestion time and block the batch on failure; partial ingestion of invalid data is more dangerous than rejecting the full batch.
Semantic versioning governs compatibility: additive optional fields are MINOR; field renames, type changes, and removals are MAJOR and require consumer coordination before the producer deploys.
PII field declarations in the contract drive masking, exclusion, and right-to-erasure propagation in the AI pipeline; undeclared PII entering the vector store is a compliance failure.
Contract tests in the producer's CI pipeline catch schema violations and quality degradation before they reach production AI pipelines; a failing contract test should be a build failure.
